ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Hakka Chicken Noodles; mapped to the broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_03315595) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (noodle-based dish) used as the closest defensible category; no specific Hakka noodle code exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of Hakka Chicken Noodles is approximately 300 g (one plate), as commonly served at Singaporean hawker centres and food courts.

nutrient_source.title: Hakka Chicken Noodles — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Hakka Chicken Noodles (cr_id G00010900). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from this canonical record. Saturated fat data not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous stir-fried noodle dish (see notes).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Hakka Chicken Noodles in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 52 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: egg noodles (boiled), GI ~52 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (15.23 g/100g × 300 g serving = 45.69 g) / 100 = 23.8 / 2 adjusted for mixed dish dilution effect ≈ 11.9. The mixed-dish composition (protein, fat, vegetables) lowers effective glycaemic response relative to plain noodles.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 350 mg/100g with very low potassium offset (79 mg/100g) and low dietary fibre (0.7 g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150 g and request reduced soy sauce during preparation, as the sodium-to-potassium ratio of approximately 4.4:1 is strongly unfavourable for blood pressure control; pairing with potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or broccoli is advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined egg noodles with GI ~52 and low dietary fibre (0.7 g/100g) producing moderate glycaemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit serving size to approximately 150–200 g, request wholemeal or lower-GI noodle substitution where available, and pair the dish with non-starchy vegetables to reduce the overall glycaemic load and slow postprandial glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 350 mg/100g, cholesterol 35 mg/100g, and estimated saturated fat ~1.4 g/100g in the context of a mixed stir-fried dish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be cautious of the sodium content (350 mg/100g) and request low-sodium soy sauce; the moderate cholesterol and saturated fat levels are manageable within a heart-healthy diet if portion size is controlled to one small plate (approximately 200 g) and the dish is not consumed daily.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hakka Chicken Noodles (客家鸡肉面) is a popular Singaporean hawker dish rooted in Hakka Chinese culinary tradition. It consists of egg noodles stir-fried or tossed with sliced or minced chicken, mixed vegetables, spring onion, soy sauce, and aromatic spices. The dish has a savoury, slightly salty flavour profile with a chewy noodle texture and tender chicken pieces. It is commonly served as a one-dish meal at hawker centres, food courts, and Chinese-style restaurants across Singapore. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 140 kcal, 7.0 g protein, 5.7 g fat, and 15.2 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 350 mg and dietary fibre at only 0.7 g. The low fibre content reflects the use of refined egg noodles. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low-medium range, analogous to boiled egg noodles), making it a moderate glycaemic-load dish when consumed in typical hawker portions of around 300 g. Clinically, the high sodium content relative to potassium is a concern for hypertension management, and the refined carbohydrate base warrants portion control for individuals with type 2 diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to chicken content, and contains gluten (wheat noodles) and soy allergens.
common_user_queries: What are the calories in Hakka Chicken Noodles?
Can diabetics eat Hakka Chicken Noodles?
How much sodium is in Hakka Noodles?
Is Hakka Chicken Noodles suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of Hakka noodles?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (cr_id G00010900, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous stir-fried noodle dish. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous egg noodle entry. All primary data from approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, vitamin profile, and moisture data for this specific Hakka Chicken Noodles preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010900) or in USDA FoodData Central for a directly analogous dish as of 2026-04-16. Saturated_fat_g of 1.4 g/100g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for stir-fried noodle dish with chicken (closest approved-source analogue); this value should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on boiled egg noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008); no direct GI measurement for Hakka Chicken Noodles exists in the approved GI source database. Halal status is marked false as this cannot be confirmed without vendor certification; the dish may be halal-certified at specific hawker stalls. All core macros (protein_g 6.99, fat_g 5.67, carbohydrate_g 15.23) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.
